expanded_description:
Why does the Short-Run Aggregate Supply curve slope upward? In classical theory, prices and wages adjust instantaneously, making output independent of the price level. Yet real economies exhibit persistent nominal rigidities: wages are locked into multi-year contracts, firms hesitate to change printed prices, and workers resist nominal pay cuts even during downturns. These stickiness frictions create a wedge between actual and expected price levels, temporarily altering real production costs and profit margins. When the aggregate price level rises unexpectedly, nominal wages and many input prices remain fixed in the short run. Real labor costs fall, profit margins expand, and firms respond by hiring more workers and increasing output. This mechanism generates the positive slope of the SRAS curve.

Understanding sticky wages and prices is essential for bridging microeconomic pricing behavior with macroeconomic output dynamics. It explains why monetary policy can influence real variables in the short run, why inflation shocks transmit unevenly across sectors, and why economies experience temporary booms and recessions rather than instantaneous market clearing. In this section, we will dissect the contractual, informational, and behavioral foundations of nominal rigidity. You will learn to quantify real wage adjustments under sticky conditions, evaluate firms' menu cost trade-offs, and trace how staggered price setting aggregates into an upward-sloping SRAS curve. Mastering these microfoundations is critical for analyzing short-run stabilization policy and recognizing the limits of demand management.

core_concept.principle:
The upward slope of the Short-Run Aggregate Supply curve arises because nominal wages ($W$) and many output prices are sticky in the short run. They adjust sluggishly to changes in the aggregate price level ($P$) due to contracts, menu costs, coordination failures, and efficiency wage considerations.

When $P$ rises while $W$ remains fixed, the real wage falls:

$\frac{W}{P} \downarrow \Rightarrow \text{Labor Cost per Unit} \downarrow \Rightarrow \text{Profit Margin} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Firms Hire More} \Rightarrow Y \uparrow$

Conversely, when $P$ falls unexpectedly, the real wage rises, squeezing profit margins and prompting production cuts. This inverse relationship between the price level and real marginal cost, mediated by nominal rigidities, generates the positive slope of SRAS. In the long run, all contracts expire, prices are reset, and expectations adjust ($W$ and $P$ rise/fall proportionally), restoring real wages to equilibrium and returning the SRAS curve to vertical at $Y^*$.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine a manufacturing firm that signed a one-year labor contract fixing hourly wages at $\$25$. Suddenly, a surge in aggregate demand pushes the prices the firm receives for its products up by $8\%$. Because the wage contract is locked in, the cost of producing each unit rises by far less than $8\%$. The profit margin per unit expands. The manager's rational response is to run machines longer, offer overtime, and temporarily hire contract workers to maximize the temporary profit opportunity.

Now scale this to the entire economy. Not all wages and prices adjust simultaneously. Some are bound by contracts, others face menu costs, and many firms wait to see if competitors will change prices first. When the overall price level rises, firms with sticky costs experience a surge in real profitability. They expand output. As more firms experience this, total real GDP rises. That is why the SRAS curve slopes upward. The stickiness is temporary, but during the adjustment window, nominal changes translate directly into real production responses.

image.alt: Diagram showing fixed nominal wage line intersecting labor demand curve, with real wage falling as P rises, leading to higher employment and output.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.1: Sticky nominal wages and prices create temporary real cost advantages when the aggregate price level changes, generating the upward slope of SRAS.

sections.introduction:
The assumption of flexible prices and wages simplifies classical models but fails to capture real-world macroeconomic dynamics. Empirical evidence consistently shows that nominal adjustments are gradual, staggered, and often asymmetric. Firms do not change prices every day; workers do not renegotiate wages monthly. These nominal rigidities are not market failures—they are rational responses to transaction costs, information constraints, and institutional arrangements. Yet they have profound macroeconomic consequences: they allow monetary policy to influence real output, they create short-run trade-offs between inflation and unemployment, and they determine the speed and welfare cost of economic adjustments.

In this section, we will explore the microeconomic foundations of sticky wages and prices. You will learn to calculate real wage changes under nominal rigidity, evaluate menu cost decisions, and understand how staggered price setting aggregates into macroeconomic supply behavior. Mastering these concepts is essential for interpreting firm-level pricing data, forecasting inflation dynamics, and designing policies that respect both short-run frictions and long-run flexibility.

introduction_image.alt: Split image: Left shows multi-year union contract signing. Right shows restaurant menu being reprinted with new prices.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 3.9.2: Nominal rigidities arise from labor contracts, menu costs, and pricing frictions that prevent instantaneous market clearing.

key_concept.definition:
Sticky wages and prices refer to the sluggish adjustment of nominal compensation and output prices to changes in economic conditions. This stickiness creates a short-run divergence between actual prices ($P$) and expected or contracted prices, causing real variables (output, employment, real wages) to respond to nominal shocks.

Key Mechanisms:
1. Contractual Rigidity: Multi-year labor contracts, union agreements, and implicit employment arrangements fix nominal wages for extended periods.
2. Menu Costs: The physical, administrative, and customer-relationship costs of changing prices make firms reluctant to adjust frequently.
3. Coordination Failures: Firms delay price changes until competitors move, creating strategic complementarity and price stickiness.
4. Efficiency Wages: Firms pay above-market nominal wages to maintain morale, productivity, and retention, resisting downward adjustments even during downturns.

The aggregate effect is captured in the SRAS equation: $Y = Y^* + \alpha(P - P^e)$. When $P \neq P^e$, stickiness causes real output to deviate from potential.
key_concept.context: John Maynard Keynes emphasized nominal wage rigidity as central to unemployment persistence. Modern New Keynesian economics (Calvo, Mankiw, Akerlof) formalized these frictions using microfoundations: Calvo pricing models random price adjustment probabilities; efficiency wage theory explains downward wage rigidity; menu cost models show how small individual frictions generate large macroeconomic fluctuations. Central banks explicitly model these rigidities in DSGE frameworks to forecast inflation and calibrate interest rate paths. The degree of stickiness determines policy effectiveness: more rigid economies respond more strongly to demand shocks but recover more slowly without intervention.

image.alt: Gantt-style chart showing wage contracts expiring at different times and firms resetting prices on staggered schedules.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.3: Staggered contracts and pricing create overlapping nominal rigidities that smooth but delay macroeconomic adjustment.

classifications.value: Multi-year agreements and labor market institutions fix nominal wages
classifications.behavior: Formal labor contracts lock in nominal wages for one to three years. Even non-union workers face implicit contracts: firms avoid frequent wage changes to maintain trust and reduce renegotiation costs. When the price level rises unexpectedly, these fixed nominal wages translate into lower real labor costs, incentivizing firms to expand employment and output. The rigidity is most pronounced downward: firms strongly resist nominal wage cuts due to morale concerns, legal constraints, and efficiency wage considerations, making recessions more prolonged than expansions.

image.alt: Timeline of expiring labor contracts with nominal wage steps, showing real wage erosion during inflation and preservation during stable prices.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.4: Contractual wage rigidity creates predictable windows of real cost advantage or disadvantage as the price level changes.

examples.explanation: A manufacturing union secures a $2\%$ annual wage increase for three years. If inflation unexpectedly jumps to $6\%$, real wages fall by $\approx 4\%$ annually. Firms hire more workers and increase output until contracts expire and wages are renegotiated.

Statutory nominal floors prevent wages from adjusting downward during downturns. Firms facing falling product prices cannot cut nominal wages, forcing employment reductions rather than real wage adjustments, prolonging output gaps.

Professional service firms rarely cut nominal base salaries during recessions, instead freezing bonuses or reducing hours. This maintains real wage stickiness, slowing SRAS adjustment and extending recovery periods.

classifications.type: Menu Costs and Price Adjustment Frictions
classifications.value: Physical and administrative costs delay output price changes
classifications.behavior: Changing prices incurs direct costs (reprinting menus, updating shelf tags, reprogramming point-of-sale systems) and indirect costs (customer confusion, perceived unfairness, competitor retaliation). Firms therefore adjust prices infrequently, keeping nominal prices fixed across small to moderate demand fluctuations. When aggregate demand rises, firms with sticky prices experience higher sales volumes at unchanged prices, prompting them to increase production rather than immediately raise prices. This quantity adjustment mechanism is a primary driver of the short-run output response to demand shocks.

image.alt: Firm decision flowchart: Compare menu cost vs. lost profit from not changing price. If lost profit > menu cost, change price; else keep fixed.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.5: Menu costs create a band of inaction where firms tolerate suboptimal prices rather than incur adjustment costs, shifting output instead.

examples.explanation: A mail-order retailer prints quarterly catalogs with fixed prices. If inflation rises mid-cycle, the retailer absorbs the margin compression temporarily and increases order fulfillment capacity rather than reprinting immediately.
Even with digital price tags, firms coordinate updates monthly to avoid customer backlash. During demand surges, they increase staffing and inventory turnover rather than adjusting prices daily.

Industrial suppliers negotiate annual price agreements with buyers. Short-term input cost spikes are absorbed or passed through gradually, causing output adjustments rather than immediate price changes.

classifications.type: Coordination Failures and Relative Price Confusion
classifications.value: Strategic complementarity and information gaps delay synchronized adjustment
characteristics: Firms wait for competitors to move
Imperfect information on aggregate vs. relative prices
classifications.behavior: Firms are reluctant to be first movers in price changes due to competitive risk. If one firm raises prices while others do not, it loses market share. This strategic complementarity creates coordination failure: even when all firms would benefit from simultaneous price adjustment, no single firm initiates it. Additionally, producers observe their own nominal price rising but cannot immediately distinguish whether it reflects economy-wide inflation (no real profit gain) or product-specific demand strength (real profit gain). This confusion leads to temporary output expansion as firms optimistically respond to nominal signals.

image.alt: Network diagram of firms connected by competitive pricing, showing delayed synchronization and temporary output responses.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.6: Coordination failures and imperfect information create staggered price adjustments that aggregate into a sloped SRAS curve.

examples.explanation: Supermarkets monitor competitors before adjusting grocery prices. During moderate inflation, they delay changes, absorbing margin compression through higher sales volume until a critical mass of competitors moves.

A machinery manufacturer sees order prices rise by $5\%$. Without immediate macro data, it assumes product-specific demand strength and ramps up production. Later, it realizes all input prices rose equally, scaling back to normal output.

E-commerce firms use automated pricing but build in response lags to avoid algorithmic price wars. Demand surges translate into inventory drawdowns and increased fulfillment rather than instantaneous price spikes.

classifications.value: Firms maintain above-market nominal wages to preserve productivity
classifications.behavior: Efficiency wage theory posits that worker productivity depends on real compensation, morale, and perceived fairness. Firms pay above-market nominal wages to reduce turnover, attract higher-quality applicants, and discourage shirking. During recessions, cutting nominal wages risks catastrophic morale collapse, increased absenteeism, and loss of institutional knowledge. Consequently, firms prefer layoffs to wage cuts, preserving nominal wage rigidity. This downward stickiness prevents real wages from clearing the labor market, prolonging unemployment and output gaps until demand recovers or prices adjust sufficiently.

image.alt: Graph showing productivity rising with real wage up to an optimal point, with firms maintaining wages above market clearing level.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.7: Efficiency wage considerations create deliberate downward nominal rigidity, prioritizing productivity stability over labor market clearing.

examples.explanation: Software firms maintain base salaries during downturns to retain top engineering talent. They reduce bonuses and freeze hiring rather than cut nominal pay, preserving long-term innovation capacity at the cost of short-run employment.

Auto plants pay $15\%$ above regional averages to ensure quality control and reduce defect rates. During demand slumps, they absorb margin compression rather than cut wages, maintaining production standards while scaling back hours.

Restaurants avoid cutting server base wages during slow seasons to maintain morale and customer service quality. They adjust staffing levels and operating hours instead, transmitting demand shocks through quantity rather than price adjustments.

principles.principle: Nominal Rigidity Creates Short-Run Non-Neutrality of Money
principles.statement: In classical models, money is neutral: changes in the money supply affect only nominal variables, leaving real output and employment unchanged. Sticky wages and prices break this neutrality in the short run. When the central bank expands the money supply, aggregate demand rises, pushing $P$ upward. Because $W$ and many input prices are temporarily fixed, real marginal costs fall, profit margins expand, and firms increase production. Real GDP rises above $Y^*$, and unemployment falls below the natural rate. This non-neutrality is temporary: as contracts expire and prices reset, real wages return to equilibrium, SRAS shifts left, and output returns to $Y^*$. The duration of non-neutrality depends entirely on the degree of nominal rigidity.
principles.application: This principle explains why monetary policy is effective in the short run but not the long run. Central banks exploit nominal rigidity to stabilize output during recessions by lowering interest rates, boosting demand, and temporarily raising $P$ relative to $W$. However, sustained accommodation only generates higher inflation once expectations adjust. Policy design must therefore account for rigidity horizons: aggressive easing works quickly in highly rigid economies but requires earlier withdrawal to prevent inflationary overshoot as contracts reset.
principles.example: During the 2020 pandemic, the Federal Reserve cut rates to zero and expanded its balance sheet. Aggregate demand contracted initially, but subsequent fiscal transfers shifted AD right. With wages and many prices locked by prior contracts, firms with recovering demand expanded output using existing capacity. Real GDP rebounded sharply in 2021-2022. As labor contracts expired in 2022-2023, nominal wages rose $5\%$-$7\%$, eroding the initial real cost advantage. SRAS shifted left, inflation persisted, and output growth normalized to trend. The initial real output boost was purely a product of nominal rigidity.

image.alt: AD-AS diagram showing monetary expansion shifting AD right, moving equilibrium up along SRAS with higher Y and P, then long-run return to Y* after SRAS shifts left.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.8: Nominal rigidities allow monetary expansions to temporarily boost real output, but long-run neutrality is restored as wages and prices fully adjust.

principles.principle: Small Menu Costs Generate Large Macroeconomic Fluctuations
principles.statement: Menu costs at the individual firm level are trivial—often fractions of a percent of revenue. Yet they generate substantial aggregate output volatility due to strategic complementarity and aggregate demand externalities. When one firm keeps its price fixed during a demand surge, it sells more, hires more workers, and pays higher wages. Those workers spend more at other firms, boosting their sales. If all firms adjusted prices immediately, this demand amplification would not occur. Because many firms face menu costs and coordinate poorly, small individual frictions multiply across the economy, producing large swings in real output. This is why even tiny menu costs justify New Keynesian models of business cycles.
principles.application: This principle informs pricing regulation, inflation targeting, and digital economy policy. In environments with high digital pricing flexibility (e-commerce, algorithmic pricing), menu costs approach zero, theoretically flattening SRAS. However, firms still build in deliberate lags to avoid consumer backlash and algorithmic instability. Central banks recognize that highly flexible pricing environments may require faster policy responses, while rigid environments allow more patient, forward-guidance-driven approaches. Understanding menu cost aggregation is essential for calibrating the optimal inflation target and assessing the real effects of supply chain digitization.
principles.example: Consider a $2\%$ demand shock. If all firms adjust prices instantly, $P$ rises $2\%$, real output is unchanged. If menu costs prevent adjustment for six months, firms sell $2\%$ more volume, hire overtime workers, and pay $1\%$ higher wages. Those workers spend at other firms, amplifying the initial shock. Aggregate output rises $1.5\%$ temporarily. The economy experiences a measurable boom-bust cycle driven not by large frictions, but by the aggregation of many small, rational pricing delays.

image.alt: Network diagram showing firm-level price stickiness amplifying through worker income and spending channels to create macroeconomic output fluctuations.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.9: Individual menu costs aggregate through income-spending feedback loops, transforming micro frictions into macroeconomic output volatility.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Real Wage Dynamics and Output Response with Sticky Nominal Wages

image.alt: Table showing nominal wage, price level, real wage, profit margin, and output over three periods.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.10: Track how fixed nominal wages interact with changing price levels to affect real costs and production decisions.

worked_examples.problem:
A manufacturing sector operates under one-year nominal wage contracts fixing average hourly wages at $W = \$28.00$. The initial price level is $P_0 = 100$. The sector's production function implies that output $Y$ responds to the profit margin $\pi$, where $\pi = P - \text{Unit Labor Cost}$. Unit Labor Cost $= W / \text{Productivity}$. Assume productivity is constant at $0.5$ units/hour, so Unit Labor Cost $= W / 0.5 = \$56.00$ per unit of output.

PeriodPrice Level ($P$)Nominal Wage ($W$)Unit Labor CostProfit Margin per Unit
Base$100.0$$\$28.00$$\$56.00$$\$44.00$
Q1$104.0$$\$28.00$$\$56.00$?
Q2$108.0$$\$28.00$$\$56.00$?


a) Calculate the profit margin per unit for Q1 and Q2.
b) Calculate the real wage ($W/P$) for each period and explain its trend.
c) If output expands by $2\%$ for every $\$2.00$ increase in profit margin, calculate the output growth rate by Q2.
d) What happens when the contract expires and wages renegotiate at $P_{new} = 108$?

step_by_step.explanation:
Q1 Margin: $P_{Q1} - \text{Unit Labor Cost} = 104.0 - 56.0 = \$48.00$.
Q2 Margin: $P_{Q2} - \text{Unit Labor Cost} = 108.0 - 56.0 = \$52.00.$

Base: $W/P = 28.00 / 100.0 = 0.280$.
Q1: $28.00 / 104.0 \approx 0.269$ (declines $3.9\%$).
Q2: $28.00 / 108.0 \approx 0.259$ (declines $7.5\%$ from base).
The real wage falls because $W$ is sticky while $P$ rises. Workers' purchasing power erodes, but firms' labor costs in real terms decline.

Margin increase Base to Q1: $\$4.00 \Rightarrow 4.0 / 2.0 \times 2\% = 4.0\%$ output growth.
Margin increase Base to Q2: $\$8.00 \Rightarrow 8.0 / 2.0 \times 2\% = 8.0\%$ output growth.
By Q2, output expands $8.0\%$ above potential due to temporary real cost advantage.

At expiration, workers demand nominal wages that restore real purchasing power: $W_{new} = W_{base} \times (P_{new} / P_{base}) = 28.00 \times 1.08 = \$30.24$.
New Unit Labor Cost $= 30.24 / 0.5 = \$60.48$.
New Profit Margin $= 108.0 - 60.48 = \$47.52$, approximately equal to the base margin adjusted for inflation. Real wage returns to $0.280$. Output contracts back to $Y^*$. The short-run boom vanishes as SRAS shifts left.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Q1: \$48.00. Q2: \$52.00. b) Real wage falls from 0.280 to 0.259 due to sticky W and rising P. c) Output grows by 8.0% by Q2. d) Wages renegotiate to \$30.24, restoring real costs and output to potential, closing the temporary gap.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Quantifying how nominal wage stickiness creates temporary real cost advantages during inflation, driving short-run output expansion until contract renegotiation restores long-run equilibrium.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating Menu Cost Decisions and Optimal Pricing Frequency

image.alt: Decision matrix comparing profit loss from fixed price vs. menu cost across different demand shock magnitudes.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.11: Determine when firms should change prices based on menu costs versus forgone profit.

worked_examples.problem:
A regional restaurant chain faces a menu cost of $\$500$ per location to redesign, print, and update digital systems for price changes. Demand analysis shows that if the optimal price increases by $\Delta P^*$, keeping prices fixed causes a profit loss of $L = 50 \times (\Delta P^*)^2$ dollars per quarter. The chain operates 20 locations.

a) Calculate the quarterly profit loss per location if the optimal price increases by $\$1.00$, $\$2.00$, and $\$3.00$.
b) At what price deviation $\Delta P^*$ does the total chain-wide loss exceed the total menu cost for all locations?
c) If demand shocks are persistent, should the firm change prices immediately or absorb the loss? Justify using the concept of price inaction bands.
d) How does this micro decision aggregate to affect the short-run output response of the broader economy?

step_by_step.step: Calculate quarterly profit loss per location
step_by_step.explanation:
$\Delta P^* = \$1.00$: $L = 50 \times (1.00)^2 = \$50.00$ per location per quarter.
$\Delta P^* = \$2.00$: $L = 50 \times (2.00)^2 = \$200.00$.
$\Delta P^* = \$3.00$: $L = 50 \times (3.00)^2 = \$450.00.$

step_by_step.step: Compare chain-wide loss to total menu cost
step_by_step.explanation:
Total Menu Cost $= 20 \text{ locations} \times \$500 = \$10,000.
Chain-wide quarterly loss for $\$1.00$: $20 \times 50 = \$1,000$. Annualized: $\$4,000$ (still below menu cost).
For $\$2.00$: Annualized loss $= 20 \times 200 \times 4 = \$16,000$ (exceeds menu cost).
Break-even deviation: Set $20 \times 50 \times (\Delta P^*)^2 \times 4 = 10,000 \Rightarrow (\Delta P^*)^2 = 2.5 \Rightarrow \Delta P^* \approx \$1.58.$

step_by_step.step: Evaluate pricing strategy and inaction band
step_by_step.explanation: For $\Delta P^* < \$1.58$, the firm absorbs the loss and keeps prices fixed, increasing sales volume to capture demand. This creates a price inaction band. For $\Delta P^* > \$1.58$, the firm changes prices. With persistent shocks, the firm will likely change prices once, then wait until the next deviation exceeds the band. This staggered adjustment contributes to nominal rigidity at the macro level.

When many firms face similar menu costs, small aggregate demand shocks translate into quantity adjustments (higher output) rather than price adjustments. This volume response generates the upward-sloping SRAS curve. Only when cumulative shocks exceed economy-wide inaction thresholds do prices adjust broadly, shifting SRAS and restoring long-run neutrality.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) $50, $200, $450 per location per quarter. b) Deviation > \$1.58 triggers chain-wide price change. c) Absorb loss within inaction band to avoid menu costs; change prices only when persistent deviation exceeds \$1.58. d) Aggregated inaction bands cause demand shocks to initially boost output rather than prices, creating the positive SRAS slope.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying menu cost theory to firm-level pricing decisions, calculating optimal adjustment thresholds, and tracing how micro pricing frictions aggregate into macroeconomic supply behavior.

process_flow.process: Identify Nominal Contract and Pricing Structures
process_flow.description: Determine the duration, coverage, and indexation clauses of labor contracts, supplier agreements, and retail pricing policies.
process_flow.mechanism: Survey union contract expiration dates, minimum wage coverage, menu cost estimates, and digital pricing flexibility across sectors.
process_flow.result: Quantified degree of nominal rigidity in wages and output prices.

image.alt: Data dashboard showing contract expiration calendar, wage settlement rates, and price change frequency metrics.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.12: Step 1 - Map the institutional and contractual landscape to assess baseline nominal rigidity.

process_flow.process: Calculate Real Cost Impact of Price Level Changes
process_flow.description: Compare actual price level movements to fixed nominal wages and input costs to determine real margin expansion or compression.
process_flow.mechanism: Real Wage $= W/P$. Real Input Cost $= \text{Nominal Input Price} / P$. Track divergence from long-run equilibrium real costs.
process_flow.result: Magnitude and direction of short-run profit margin shifts driving output decisions.

image.alt: Formula visualization showing P rising while W remains fixed, causing real cost decline and margin expansion.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.13: Step 2 - Quantify how price level changes alter real production costs when nominal variables are sticky.

process_flow.process: Map to Firm Output and Employment Responses
process_flow.description: Translate real margin changes into production adjustments using firm-level cost curves and capacity utilization data.
process_flow.mechanism: Margin expansion $\Rightarrow$ hire overtime, defer maintenance, increase throughput. Margin compression $\Rightarrow$ cut hours, lay off workers, reduce orders.
process_flow.result: Predicted short-run output and employment response at sector and aggregate levels.

image.alt: Firm production decision flowchart linking real margins to hiring, capacity utilization, and output volume.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.14: Step 3 - Connect real cost shifts to microeconomic production decisions that drive short-run aggregate supply.

process_flow.process: Project Contract Expiration and Price Reset Timeline
process_flow.description: Estimate when nominal rigidities will unwind based on contract expiration schedules, menu cost thresholds, and expectation adjustments.
process_flow.mechanism: Track proportion of economy under active contracts each quarter. Model price reset probability using Calvo or state-dependent pricing frameworks.
process_flow.result: Forecasted SRAS adjustment speed and duration of output gap persistence.

image.alt: Timeline showing overlapping contract expirations and staggered price resets converging toward full adjustment.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.15: Step 4 - Project the unwinding of nominal rigidities to forecast the transition back to long-run equilibrium.

process_flow.process: Evaluate Policy Interaction and Stabilization Needs
process_flow.description: Determine whether monetary or fiscal policy should accommodate, accelerate, or counteract the natural adjustment process.
process_flow.mechanism: If rigidity is high and output gap large $\Rightarrow$ stimulate AD to utilize idle capacity. If inflation expectations unanchor $\Rightarrow$ tighten to restore credibility. If adjustment is rapid $\Rightarrow$ passive stance optimal.
process_flow.result: Evidence-based policy recommendation aligned with rigidity horizons and welfare trade-offs.

image.alt: Policy decision matrix weighing rigidity duration, gap severity, and expectation stability against intervention timing.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.16: Step 5 - Align stabilization policy with the pace and completeness of nominal adjustment to maximize welfare.

image.alt: Comprehensive diagram linking contractual rigidity, menu costs, coordination failures, and efficiency wages to SRAS slope and short-run output dynamics.
image.caption: Figure 3.9.17: Master framework for understanding how nominal rigidities generate the upward-sloping SRAS curve and shape short-run macroeconomic fluctuations.

summary:
Sticky wages and prices are the microeconomic foundation of the upward-sloping Short-Run Aggregate Supply curve. Multi-year labor contracts, menu costs, coordination failures, and efficiency wage considerations prevent instantaneous adjustment of nominal compensation and output prices to changes in the aggregate price level. When $P$ rises unexpectedly while $W$ remains fixed, real labor costs fall, profit margins expand, and firms increase production and employment. This temporary real effect generates the positive SRAS slope and allows monetary policy to influence real output in the short run.

The degree of stickiness determines the magnitude and duration of output responses to demand shocks. Small individual menu costs aggregate through income-spending feedback loops to create significant macroeconomic volatility. Downward nominal rigidity prolongs recessions, while upward rigidity moderates inflationary booms. As contracts expire, prices reset, and expectations adjust, real wages return to equilibrium, SRAS shifts, and output converges back to $Y^*$. Understanding nominal rigidities is essential for designing stabilization policy that respects short-run frictions while targeting long-run equilibrium, and for recognizing why temporary demand management can yield real output gains before long-run monetary neutrality is restored.
